visits.text: The journey from Manila to Banaue is an adventure in itself — a 9-hour overnight bus through the Luzon cordillera. The bus leaves Manila in the evening and arrives in Banaue at dawn, in Ifugao province. You wake up at altitude, surrounded by green mountains and mist.

The Banaue Rice Terraces have been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1995. Carved into the mountain slopes by the Ifugao people over 2,000 years ago, they rise 1,500 meters up the hillsides. The earth and stone walls, maintained by hand, represent an engineering feat that has endured for millennia.

Banaue's main viewpoint, five minutes from town by tricycle, offers the classic panoramic view of the terraces. Early morning, when the rice paddies are shrouded in mist, the landscape feels otherworldly. If the terraces are flooded (planting season, February-March), the sky's reflection in the water adds another dimension.

Banaue's small museum displays Ifugao farming tools, woven textiles, and wooden sculptures (bulul, guardians of rice granaries). It's modest but instructive for understanding the culture that created these terraces.

Climb back from Batad to the saddle point (45 minutes of steep ascent in the morning — leave early to avoid the heat), then jeepney to Banaue. If you have energy, detour to the Hapao rice terraces in Hungduan, less known but equally beautiful — and nearly deserted.

Visit to an Ifugao weaving workshop. Village women weave on backstrap looms — an ancestral technique. The textiles with red and black geometric patterns tell stories of war, fertility, and social status.
Last look at the terraces from Banaue viewpoint in late afternoon. The low-angle light carves shadows into the terraces — it's the best time for photos.

activities.text: Rest and acclimatization after the night bus. Banaue sits at 1,200 meters elevation — the air is cool morning and evening (15-20°C), a welcome contrast after Manila's heat. Grab a coffee at one of the small downtown restaurants.
Ifugao dinner: red rice from the terraces (local variety with a nutty taste) accompanied by pinikpikan — chicken broth prepared using the traditional method. Ifugao cuisine is simple and comforting.

Meeting with an Ifugao family if your guide can arrange it. The elders share stories of life in the rice fields, cañao rituals (community festivals), and the fragile balance between tradition and modernity. Young people leave for Manila, and maintaining the terraces requires hands.
Overnight bus back to Manila (departs late afternoon, arrives early morning). You'll connect directly to your flight to Palawan.

days_plan.itin_title: Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Bus then ferry from Cebu City to Bantayan Island, northwest of Cebu - count on 5-6 hours total. Bantayan is the island where Filipinos themselves go when they want beaches without crowds. The sand is white and powdery, the water calm and warm, accommodations simple and cheap.

Sugar Beach in Santa Fe is the main beach - a kilometer of fine sand bordered by a few discreet resorts. The water is so shallow you walk 100 meters before it reaches waist-deep. No jet-skis, no banana boats - just the sea.
Bantayan Town village, west of the island, has a 17th-century coral church (Saints Peter and Paul Church) and a lively fish market. The streets are quiet, tricycles rare, dogs numerous.

activities.text: Island scooter tour. Bantayan is 15 km long - in two hours you've seen everything: deserted beaches on the north side, mangroves on the east, fishing villages. Stop at Ogtong Cave, a natural freshwater turquoise cave-pool.
Sunset at Kota Beach, west coast. The beach is narrow but the view is clear and the sky colors are spectacular.

days_plan.itin_title: Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days

visits.text: Batad is the hidden jewel of the rice terraces — a natural amphitheater of terraces forming a semicircle, more spectacular and better preserved than Banaue. Access is via a 45-minute downhill trail from the saddle point (jeepney drop-off). The path crosses rice paddies where farmers work knee-deep in mud.

Batad village is a handful of wooden and corrugated iron houses clinging to the slope. No road, no cars — everything's done on foot. Ifugao families have lived in the same thatch-roofed houses for generations. Some offer homestays: a mat on the floor, a meal of red rice and vegetables, and mountain silence.

Tappiya Falls, accessible from Batad via a steep 40-minute descent (and an equally steep ascent — prepare your legs), drops 21 meters into a cold-water pool amid the jungle. The swim after the hike is pure relief.

activities.text: Rice terrace trek with a local guide. The trail winds between terraces, crosses stone walls, and follows ancient irrigation channels. The guide explains the hydraulic system, planting rituals, and the contemporary challenges facing Ifugao farmers.
Quiet evening in Batad. No wifi (or barely), no bars, no noise — just roosters crowing, water murmuring through the channels, and stars above the terraces.

days_plan.itin_title: Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Cebu City is the Philippines' oldest city - this is where Magellan landed in 1521, and where he died just weeks later, killed by Chief Lapu-Lapu on Mactan Island across the strait. The city preserves traces of this turbulent history.

Fort San Pedro, built by the Spanish in 1565, is the country's oldest fort. It's a modest stone triangle heavy with history - it served as a Spanish garrison, a Japanese prison camp, and even a zoo before being restored. The interior gardens are peaceful and shaded.

The Basilica of Santo Niño houses the Philippines' oldest Christian relic - a statue of the Child Jesus given by Magellan in 1521. The church is always packed with devotees lighting candles and praying aloud. The atmosphere is intense and sincere.

Right next door, Magellan's Cross - or rather its shrine. The original cross planted by Magellan in 1521 is said to be inside a cross made of tindalo wood, itself protected by an octagonal pavilion with a painted ceiling. Historians debate its authenticity, but devotees don't care and come to pray.

The Temple of Leah, in the hills above Cebu, is a neo-Roman mausoleum built by a businessman for his deceased wife. Corinthian columns, golden statues, monumental staircases - it's kitsch, grandiose and touching all at once. The view over the city from the terrace is worth the trip.

Continue climbing to Tops Lookout, the highest viewpoint accessible by vehicle above Cebu City. From the platform, the 360° panorama sweeps across the city, Cebu Strait, Mactan Island and the interior mountains. Best in late afternoon when the heat haze lifts.

The Taoist Temple, in the Beverly Hills residential district (yes, like Los Angeles), is a colorful Chinese temple open to visitors. Red and green pagodas, carved dragons, and a clear view over the city. Cebu's Chinese community is old and influential - this temple is its symbol.
Ferry from Dumaguete to Liloan (south coast of Cebu), then drive north up the coast. Total journey to Cebu City takes 5-6 hours with stops.

Possible stop in Oslob. This is where whale sharks are fed by fishermen to allow tourists to swim with them. The site is controversial - feeding alters these wild animals' behavior and attracts massive crowds. If you go, know that the practice is criticized by marine conservation NGOs.
Alternative to Oslob: Sumilon Island, right across the water, offers a white sandbar surrounded by turquoise water and a preserved reef for snorkeling - without ethical controversy.

Heading back up to Cebu City, stop at the Simala Sanctuary (Monastery of the Holy Eucharist). This neo-Gothic church-fortress, perched in the hills, is a major pilgrimage site for Filipinos. The architecture is extravagant - turrets, stained glass, monumental staircases. A place of intense faith.
Return to Cebu from Bantayan - same ferry + bus route, 5-6 hours. If your flight is in the evening, you have time for one last stop on Mactan Island, across from Cebu City.

The Lapu-Lapu Shrine, on Mactan beach, marks the spot where the warrior chief killed Magellan on April 27, 1521. A bronze monument depicts Lapu-Lapu as a national hero - the Philippines' first resistance fighter against colonizers. Irony: right across from it, an obelisk honors Magellan. Both sides commemorated side by side.

Mactan's guitar factories: the island is the Philippines' main producer of handcrafted guitars. The workshops (Alegre, Lapu-Lapu Guitar) shape ukuleles and guitars from local wood - you can visit and buy directly.

activities.text: Stroll through Carbon Market, Cebu's largest and oldest market. It's loud, dense, chaotic - and fascinating. Mountains of mangoes, hundreds of dried fish, medicinal herbs, hardware goods. Not a tourist market, a real city market.

Lechon dinner in Cebu - the city considers itself the roast suckling pig capital of the Philippines. The skin crackles when you bite it, the meat is infused with herbs and lemongrass. Ask for a local restaurant rather than a chain.

Evening at IT Park - Cebu's business district that comes alive at night. The call centers work on American hours, so restaurants and food courts stay open until 3am. Young, modern atmosphere, very different from the historic center.
If you like live music, Filipinos are formidable singers. Cebu's karaoke bars aren't tourist traps - it's genuine national culture. Any tricycle driver knows a good bar.
Scenic drive along Cebu's southeast coast. The road winds between sea and mountains, passing fishing villages and viewpoints over the Bohol Strait.
Reunion dinner in Cebu City - the loop is complete. Return to lechon or discover satti (chicken and beef skewers in coconut sauce, a Mindanao specialty adopted by Cebu).

Last-minute shopping: dried mangoes from Cebu (the country's best), otap (flaky biscuits), and rosquillos (dry donuts). Mactan-Cebu Airport is international - direct flights to Manila, Palawan, and several Asian cities.

days_plan.itin_title: Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Arrival at Busuanga Airport, a 30-minute drive from Coron Town. The van crosses a countryside of green hills and water buffalo before reaching the small town, wedged between the sea and towering limestone cliffs. Coron is no beach resort - it's a fishing port that's adapted to tourism without losing its soul.

Coron Town's market comes alive in late afternoon. Fishermen unload the day's catch - tuna, marlin, giant prawns - which the waterfront restaurants grill to order for dinner. Try the local cashew nuts, a Palawan specialty, sold in bags on every street corner.

Coron's alleyways are simple - concrete, corrugated iron and hand-painted signs. But that authenticity is the charm. No international chains, no shopping malls. A few local bakeries (panaderias) sell sweet bread still warm for a handful of pesos.

Day trip by bangka (traditional outrigger boat) around Coron Island. First stop: Kayangan Lake. After a short climb over the limestone rocks, the view of the lake below takes your breath away - a bowl of emerald water ringed by black cliffs draped in vegetation. The descent leads to a floating platform from which you dive into water so clear the bottom is visible at 15 meters.

Twin Lagoon, two pools of turquoise water connected by a narrow passage between the cliffs. At low tide, you swim through an opening in the rock; at high tide, you climb a wooden ladder and descend the other side. The water in the second pool is warmer - underground springs heat it.

Barracuda Lake, deeper and stranger. The water goes from 28°C at the surface to 38°C at depth - a mixture of fresh and salt water that creates convection currents visible to the naked eye. If you dive below 5 meters, the heat becomes palpable. The lake takes its name from a large solitary barracuda that used to live there.

Final stop at Coral Garden, a reef accessible by snorkeling from the bangka. The corals are in good condition and the variety of tropical fish is impressive - clownfish, damselfish, parrotfish, surgeonfish.

Coron is one of the best wreck diving sites in the world. In September 1944, the US Navy sank about a dozen Japanese ships sheltering in the bay. Several of these wrecks rest between 10 and 40 meters deep, accessible by scuba diving. The Irako, a 147-meter supply ship, and the Akitsushima, a seaplane tender, are the most impressive.

For non-divers, the Skeleton Wreck lies at just 5 meters depth - visible by snorkeling in calm weather. The hull overgrown with corals and sponges attracts schools of fish. It's a strange experience to float above a warship looking down through the water.
CYC Beach, a small white sand beach on an islet 20 minutes by bangka, offers a break between dives. The water is calm, the bottom sandy, and the island barely 200 meters long.

activities.text: Climb the 721 steps of Mount Tapyas before sunset. The ascent is steep but short (20 minutes). At the summit, a white cross overlooks the entire bay of Coron - the limestone islands stand silhouetted against a sky turning orange. It's the best viewpoint in the area, and it's free.

Grilled fish dinner on the waterfront. The restaurants lined up facing the harbor all work the same way: you choose your fish from the display, they grill it over charcoal. The price is negotiated by weight.
Lunch served on the bangka between stops - rice, grilled fish, fruit. The boatmen prepare the meal on a small grill at the back of the boat while you swim. Simple and perfect.
Free snorkeling around the reefs. If you don't have your own equipment, the bangkas provide it - quality varies, better to rent a good mask in Coron Town before the excursion.
Scuba diving on one or two wrecks (for certified divers). Coron's dive centers offer full-day or half-day trips. Book the day before, especially in high season (December-March).

Late afternoon, head to Maquinit Hot Springs, 30 minutes by tuk-tuk from Coron Town. These hot saltwater springs (38-40°C) are nestled among the rocks by the sea. The main pool faces the sunset - a perfect moment of relaxation after a day of diving.

days_plan.itin_title: Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Ferry from Siquijor to Dumaguete - 1 hour crossing. Dumaguete is nicknamed "City of Gentle People." It's a lively university town but never stressful, with a pleasant waterfront boulevard and a restaurant and café scene driven by the student community.

Rizal Boulevard, the seafront promenade, is the heart of Dumaguete. Locals gather here in the evening to walk, eat and watch the sunset over Siquijor Island across the water. Food stalls serve cheap barbecue and vegetable tempura.

Silliman University, founded in 1901 by American Presbyterian missionaries, is Asia's oldest Protestant campus. The white period buildings, lawns and coconut wood church are worth a stroll - it's calm and shaded.

The Twin Lakes (Balinsasayao and Danao), 45 minutes into the mountains from Dumaguete, are two crater lakes nestled in dense rainforest at 900 meters elevation. The water is black and still, vegetation drips from the banks, the silence is total. You'd think you were in a different country.

Casaroro Falls, near Valencia village (20 min from Dumaguete), tumbles 30 meters into a narrow fern-lined canyon. Access is via a 300-step staircase - the descent is easy, the climb back up less so. The pool water is cold and the current strong - be careful.

activities.text: Excursion to Apo Island, 45 minutes by bangka from Malatapay (30 min drive from Dumaguete). It's a 72-hectare marine sanctuary where green turtles are so numerous you sometimes see 10 in a single dive. Snorkeling from the beach is already excellent.
Dinner on Rizal Boulevard. The waterfront terrace restaurants are affordable and the food is good - try kinilaw (Filipino ceviche with coconut vinegar).

Kayaking on Lake Balinsasayao. Kayaks are available for rent on site. You paddle on mirror-black water surrounded by tree ferns and bird songs. The second lake, Danao, is accessible via a 20-minute forest trail.
If it's a Wednesday, stop by Malatapay market on the way to the lakes. It's a noisy and colorful livestock and fish market - pigs, goats, dried fish, tropical fruits. Authentic and impressive.

days_plan.itin_title: Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days

visits.text: The crossing from Coron to El Nido takes between 3.5 and 7 hours depending on the type of boat - speed boat or slow bangka. The open sea journey can be rough, especially during the monsoon (June-October). If you get seasick, take the speed boat and medication before departure.

El Nido appears behind a curtain of black limestone cliffs rising above the sea. The contrast is striking: vertical rock spires 200 meters high drop straight into turquoise water. The small town stretches along a beige sand beach lined with restaurants and dive shops.

Walk down El Nido's main street in late afternoon. It's lively without being overwhelming - tricycles (motorbikes with sidecars), stray dogs, children playing basketball on a concrete court, and travelers from around the world sitting at terrace tables.

Island hopping day - Tour A, the most popular in El Nido. First stop: Big Lagoon, an arm of sea enclosed between towering cliffs. The bangka stops at the entrance and you continue by kayak through an increasingly narrow channel. The water shifts from deep blue to jade green in a matter of meters.

Small Lagoon, right next door. You access it by swimming under a rock arch. On the other side, an enclosed pool surrounded by limestone walls - it feels like a movie set. Tropical fish swim between your legs in warm, transparent water.

Secret Lagoon (Secret Beach), accessible through a hole in the cliff. You swim through an opening in the rock and emerge onto a tiny hidden beach, surrounded by walls on all sides - a circle of white sand and turquoise water invisible from outside.
Shimizu Island, an islet planted with coconut palms and a coral reef accessible by snorkeling. Lunch is often served here - on the beach, in the shade of the trees.

Nacpan Beach, 45 minutes north of El Nido by scooter along a potholed road (improved in recent years but still bumpy in places). The detour is worth it: 4 kilometers of golden sand almost deserted, lined with coconut palms. No concrete resorts, just a few bamboo huts and hammocks. It's the beach that postcards don't show because there's nothing on it - and that's exactly the point.

At the end of Nacpan, a sandbar connects to the neighboring beach of Calitang - the famous Twin Beach. One side has choppy water (ocean side), the other is flat (bay side). You walk between the two waters on a strip of sand 50 meters wide.

Las Cabanas Beach, south of El Nido, is where travelers gather for sunset. A zipline connects the beach to a small islet opposite - a popular attraction but not mandatory. The beach itself is pleasant, with bars right on the sand.

activities.text: Sunset from Corong-Corong Beach, a 10-minute tricycle ride south of El Nido. The beach is quieter than the town center, and the view of the limestone cliffs illuminated by the evening light is remarkable.

Dinner in one of the main street restaurants. Filipino cuisine is underrated - try the kare-kare (oxtail stew in peanut sauce) or sinigang (sour tamarind soup). Portions are generous and prices modest.
Kayaking in the Big Lagoon. Kayaks are available for rent on site - expect 200-300 PHP. Paddle to the back of the lagoon where the water becomes so shallow you can see fish darting under the hull.
Snorkeling around Shimizu Island. The corals are colorful and well-preserved, and visibility often exceeds 15 meters. Watch out for jellyfish during the wet season (June-October).

Scooter tour through El Nido's hinterland. Behind the coastal cliffs, the landscape changes: terraced rice fields, water buffalo in the paddies, villages of bamboo and corrugated iron where children wave as you pass. It's another face of Palawan, far from the turquoise lagoons.
Sunset at Las Cabanas with a drink in a beach bar. The atmosphere is relaxed, the music mellow, and the sun drops behind the limestone islets.

days_plan.itin_title: Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Flight from Dumaguete or Cebu to Siargao (via Manila or Cebu — connections vary, check ahead). Siargao is the Philippines' surf capital, but it's also a beautiful island for non-surfers: lagoons, mangroves, deserted islets.
General Luna, the main village, is a single street lined with restaurants, surf shops, and bars. The vibe recalls Canggu in Bali 10 years ago — relaxed, young, international, not yet overrun.

Cloud 9, 10 minutes from General Luna by scooter, is Siargao's legendary surf spot. A hollow wave breaking over a coral reef — reserved for experienced surfers. From the wooden boardwalk, you watch surfers charge the wave. Even without surfing, the show is worth it.

Sugba Lagoon, in the island's northwest, is a turquoise lagoon ringed by mangroves. Access is by bangka from Del Carmen (45 min drive + 30 min boat). Jumping platforms (10 meters) let you dive into the warm, calm water. Paddleboarding, kayaking, and snorkeling available onsite.

Magpupungko Rock Pools, on the east coast, are natural pools that only appear at low tide. Flat rock formations create crystal-clear basins in the middle of the reef — you swim in a natural aquarium. Check tide times before you go.
Island hopping: Siargao's three islands. Naked Island is a bare sandbar in the middle of the ocean — not a tree, not a shade, just white sand and blue water. Bring a hat.
Daku Island, the largest of the three, offers coconut palms for shade and a living reef for snorkeling. This is where lunch happens — fishermen grill the morning catch on the beach.
Guyam Island, a tiny islet of coconut palms (you can walk around it in 5 minutes), is Siargao's postcard. Robinson Crusoe meets tropical paradise.

activities.text: If you surf, this is the moment. Cloud 9 for advanced, neighboring beaches (Jacking Horse, Stimpy's) for intermediates. Surf lessons are offered everywhere for beginners — the inside waves are mellow and accessible.
Sunset from a General Luna bar. Live music, cocktails, friendly atmosphere — Siargao in the evening is a surf village where everyone knows each other after two days.

Scooter ride on the Coconut Road, a route that crosses kilometers of coconut plantations in the island's center. The palms form a tunnel of greenery — it's one of the Philippines' most beautiful roads and it's nearly deserted.
Late afternoon surf session or free swim. Siargao's pace is slow — no one will rush you.
Snorkeling around all three islands. The reefs are alive and colorful, fish abundant.
Last sunset in Siargao. The golden light on the coconut palms, the sound of waves, distant music from a bar — this is the moment to realize that three weeks in the Philippines isn't enough.

days_plan.itin_title: Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Manila is a city you either embrace or flee — loud, dense, chaotic, yet full of surprises. Intramuros, the walled city fortified by the Spanish in the 16th century, makes the best starting point. Stone walls encircle a district of quiet alleys, churches, and shaded courtyards — an island of calm within the megacity.

Fort Santiago, at the entrance to Intramuros, served as the Spanish citadel and later as a Japanese prison during the war. José Rizal, the Philippine national hero, was imprisoned here before his execution in 1896. The museum traces his final hours — his writings, his footsteps marked from the cell to the execution site.

San Agustin Church, built in 1607, is the oldest stone church in the Philippines and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The interior is breathtaking: trompe-l'oeil ceilings, crystal chandeliers, and granite floors imported from China. It survived earthquakes, typhoons, and the 1945 bombardment.

Rizal Park (Luneta Park) stretches along Manila Bay. This is where Rizal was executed by firing squad in 1896 — a granite monument marks the spot. Filipinos come here to jog in the morning, picnic with family on weekends, and watch the sunset in the evening.

Binondo, Manila's Chinatown, is the oldest Chinatown in the world — founded in 1594. The narrow streets overflow with restaurants, apothecaries, temples, and jewelry shops. Taste the siopao (steamed buns), lumpia (Filipino spring rolls), and pancit canton (stir-fried noodles).

Quiapo Church houses the Black Nazarene, a dark wooden statue of Christ carrying the cross. On January 9th, millions of barefoot devotees pull the statue through the streets in one of the world's largest religious processions. Outside the festival, the church remains a place of intense devotion.

The National Museum of Fine Arts, in a neoclassical building facing Rizal Park, displays Juan Luna's Spoliarium — a monumental 7-by-4-meter canvas that won the gold medal at the 1884 Paris Salon. Admission is free.

Flight Siargao → Manila (1.5-2h depending on connections). Return to the megacity after three weeks of islands and villages. The shock is abrupt — traffic jams, horns, urban heat. But Manila has its own energy that you come to appreciate.

If you have a few hours before your international flight, Greenbelt in Makati is an air-conditioned shopping mall-garden where you can eat, drink coffee, and buy last-minute souvenirs in modern comfort.

activities.text: Calesa ride through Intramuros — a horse-drawn carriage tour along the cobbled streets. Touristy but pleasant for covering the district without walking in the heat. Negotiate the price before you climb aboard.

Filipino dinner: adobo (chicken or pork braised in vinegar and soy), sinigang (sour tamarind soup), and halo-halo (shaved ice dessert with beans, jellies, and condensed milk). Halo-halo looks bizarre at first glance and becomes addictive after the first spoonful.

In the evening, explore Poblacion in Makati — the former working-class neighborhood turned nightlife hub. Rooftop bars, fusion restaurants, and art galleries in converted garages. The vibe is young and creative.
Sunset over Manila Bay from Rizal Boulevard or a Makati rooftop bar. Manila's sky takes on spectacular orange hues — pollution has at least this advantage.
Last Filipino meal: back to halo-halo to come full circle. Or adobo, sinigang, lechon — everything you discovered in three weeks, one last time.
Transfer to NAIA airport. Allow plenty of time — traffic from Manila to the airport can take 2 hours during rush hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Drive from Cebu City to Moalboal on the southwest coast - 3 hours by bus or van through the mountains. The landscape shifts from dense city to jungle, then sugarcane fields and coastal villages.

Moalboal is a divers' village. No fine sandy beach here - the Panagsama shore is rocky and lined with bars and dive centers. What draws people is what's happening underwater: 5 meters from shore, the reef drops vertically into deep blue.

White Beach, 10 minutes by scooter from Panagsama, is Moalboal's real beach - white sand, coconut palms, calm water. Much less crowded than northern Cebu's beaches, it offers a simple and pleasant spot to relax.

Pescador Island, 20 minutes by bangka from Moalboal, is a vegetation-covered rock sitting in the middle of the strait. Underwater, it's a cathedral: the reef descends in vertical walls carpeted with soft corals, gorgonians and sponges. Green turtles reside around the island year-round - you'll almost certainly encounter one.

Kawasan Falls, 30 minutes' drive south of Moalboal (Badian village), are among the Philippines' most beautiful. The turquoise water tumbles in two tiers into a jungle-surrounded pool. The water's almost surreal blue comes from the limestone it flows through.

activities.text: First snorkeling at Panagsama: enter the water from shore and swim 20 meters - you hit the Sardine Run. A school of several million sardines forms a shifting silver wall right at the drop-off edge. The sardines constantly change shape like a single organism. It's free, accessible to everyone, and stunning.
Dinner at one of Panagsama Beach's restaurants. The vibe is laid-back - divers comparing their days, solo travelers reading, reggae music in the background. The grilled fish is fresh and cheap.

Canyoneering to Kawasan Falls - the region's star activity. You work your way up the canyon for 3-4 hours, jumping from rock to rock, swimming through natural pools and jumping off cliffs (3 to 15 meters) into turquoise water. Intense, wet, and memorable.

Return to Moalboal exhausted but happy. One last snorkel at sunset with the sardines - the school moves differently depending on the time and light. At night, the sardines rise toward the surface and shimmer under the lights.

days_plan.itin_title: Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: The journey from El Nido to Port Barton by van takes 3 to 4 hours on a road that cuts through Palawan's jungle. The first half is paved, the second becomes a track - bumps guaranteed. But the scenery compensates: dense tropical forest, rivers, isolated villages.

Port Barton is the antithesis of El Nido. A village of 200 inhabitants, a sandy beach lined with coconut palms, no paved road, no ATM (bring cash), no nightlife. The wifi is hit-or-miss. It's exactly what those who come here are looking for.
Walk along the main beach in late afternoon. Fishermen mend their nets, dogs sleep in the shade, a few travelers read in hammocks. At the end of the beach, a path leads to a small isolated cove.

Bangka excursion around the Port Barton islands - the calm, intimate version of El Nido's island hopping. No crowds here: your bangka will often be the only one on site. Exotic Island, a white sandbar ringed with turquoise water, is the first stop.
German Island, larger, has a shaded beach and a reef accessible in a few strokes. The clownfish are so unafraid they come to inspect you up close. The water is stunningly clear.

Starfish Island takes its name from the starfish that populate the shallow waters around the islet. Don't take them out of the water (they die within minutes in the air) - observe them where they are, in 30 cm of water.

activities.text: First swim in Port Barton bay. The water is calm, warm, and sea turtles regularly come to swim near the shore - especially early morning and late afternoon.
Dinner in one of the village's small eateries. The choice is limited but fresh: grilled fish, rice, stir-fried vegetables. Some evenings, a guitarist plays Filipino ballads under the stars.
Snorkeling around the islands. Port Barton's reefs are among the best-preserved in Palawan - less frequented than El Nido's, they harbor remarkable coral diversity. Green turtles frequently spotted.
Lunch on a deserted beach, prepared by the boatman: grilled fish, green mango, rice. Feet in the sand, alone on the island.

days_plan.itin_title: Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days
Palawan Circuit: Coron to Puerto Princesa in 12 Days

visits.text: Route heading south toward Sabang - the starting point for Puerto Princesa's underground river, a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1999. The journey from Port Barton takes 4 to 5 hours by van, with a possible stop in Sabang for the night or as a day trip from Puerto Princesa (1.5 hours drive).

Puerto Princesa's underground river burrows 8.2 kilometers under a limestone mountain - it's one of the longest navigable underground rivers in the world. The visit is by paddled boat for the first 1.5 kilometers. The guide illuminates the walls with a flashlight: stalactites, stalagmites, thousands of bats, and rock formations that resemble cathedrals.
Sabang Beach, right across from the boat landing, is a pleasant surprise. Fine sand, clear water, thick jungle in the background. Few people outside the river tour hours.

Puerto Princesa is Palawan's capital - a sprawling provincial city with no great architectural charm, but lively and functional. The main street (Rizal Avenue) concentrates restaurants, shops and local life. This is where you return to civilization after several days in isolated villages.

Puerto Princesa's central market is worth a morning visit. Tropical fruits in abundance (Palawan mangoes, considered the best in the Philippines), dried fish, spices. The atmosphere is noisy and colorful.

Baker's Hill, on the outskirts of town, is a kitschy but enjoyable garden park, famous for its hopia (filled pastries) and cookies. Filipinos love it - it's an almost obligatory stop for them. The view over the valley from the terrace is pleasant.

The Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception, in central Puerto Princesa, is a city landmark - white and blue façade, cool and quiet interior. Filipinos are deeply Catholic and the churches are rarely empty.

Take a tricycle to Iwahig, 20 minutes from the center. The river winds through dense mangrove where you can travel by paddleboard or kayak. The ecosystem is rich - herons, kingfishers, mangrove crabs. It's calm, green, and the opposite of the turquoise blue of previous days.

The Palawan Butterfly Eco-Garden, a small botanical garden on the outskirts of town, houses butterflies endemic to Palawan and a collection of medicinal plants used by indigenous peoples. A short but instructive visit.

Last morning in Puerto Princesa. If your flight is in the afternoon, take advantage of the morning to stroll one last time along Rizal Avenue. The sidewalks are lively from 7am - vendors selling pan de sal (warm bread rolls), local coffee stands, schoolchildren in uniform.

Puerto Princesa Airport is 5 minutes from the city center - one of the few Philippine airports this accessible. Direct flights to Manila (1h20), Cebu (1h30) or Clark. If you're continuing to the Visayas, the flight to Cebu avoids going back through Manila.

activities.text: Underground river tour by boat. Reserve your permit in advance - the number of visitors is limited to 900 per day. The tour lasts 45 minutes in near-total darkness, lulled by the sound of water and the beating of bat wings.
Swimming at Sabang Beach after the tour. The water is warm, the bottom sandy, and monitor lizards (large lizards) stroll calmly on the beach - harmless but impressive.

Excursion to Honda Bay, 30 minutes north of Puerto Princesa. Island hopping between three or four islets - Starfish Island, Luli Island, Cowrie Island. Less spectacular than Coron or El Nido, but the water is beautiful and the reefs alive.

Dinner on Rizal Avenue. Try the crocodile sisig (yes, chopped crocodile sisig-style) - a local specialty, a bit touristy but original. Otherwise, grilled chicken inasal is a Filipino classic that's always reliable.

Firefly watching on the Iwahig River at dusk. By boat, you go up the river in darkness - and the mangroves light up. Thousands of fireflies blink in synchrony in the trees, like living fairy lights. It's an astonishing natural spectacle, especially in the dry season.

Last Filipino dinner. If you haven't tried lechon (spit-roasted suckling pig) yet, now's the time. The skin is crispy as glass, the meat melt-in-your-mouth. It's the ultimate celebration dish in the Philippines.
Last purchases: Palawan cashew nuts (the best in the country), dried mangoes, and why not a bottle of lambanog (coconut alcohol). The souvenir shops on Rizal Avenue have honest prices.

visits.text: Ferry from Tagbilaran to Siquijor - 2 hour crossing. Siquijor is the most mysterious of the Visayas islands. Filipinos call it "the island of fire": early Spanish navigators saw glows in the night (probably fireflies) and thought the island was haunted. Even today, Siquijor is renowned for its traditional healers and potions.

The town of Siquijor, around the port, is tiny and peaceful. The white and clean Saint Francis of Assisi Church dates from the 19th century. Next door, a converted convent museum displays artifacts from the colonial era.

Capilay Spring Park, 10 minutes from the center, is a freshwater pool fed by a natural spring. Locals come to bathe with family on weekends. The water is cool, clear, and the park is shaded - a simple and pleasant stop.

Cambugahay Falls, in the island's interior, cascade in three tiers into turquoise pools. A rope swing system lets you fling yourself into the water from the bank - fun and refreshing. Access is easy, 10 minutes walking downhill.

The Balete Tree, a centuries-old banyan fig with tentacle-like aerial roots, shelters a natural pool at its base where small fish nibble dead skin off your feet (natural fish spa). The tree is impressive - its roots form a network of organic columns.

Lazi Church and Convent, built in 1857 from coral stone and hardwood, form one of the Visayas' finest colonial ensembles. The convent, enormous for such a small island, testifies to the ambition of the Augustinian Recollects who administered the island.

Salagdoong Beach, on the east coast, is famous for its jumping platforms - 5 and 10 meters above deep, clear water. Even if you don't jump, the beach is beautiful and the water perfect for snorkeling.

activities.text: Scooter tour to get a feel for the island. Siquijor is 20 km in diameter - the complete loop takes 2 hours without stopping. The coastal road passes deserted beaches, village churches, giant trees (the centuries-old balete tree) and views of Bohol and Negros.
Sunset from Paliton Beach on the west coast. A row of coconut palms lean over a narrow white sand beach - the island's most photogenic natural frame.

If you're curious, ask to meet a traditional healer (mananambal). These practitioners use plants, prayers and rituals to treat physical and spiritual ailments. The healers' festival is held every Holy Week in San Antonio. Outside this period, encounters are possible but discreet - ask your accommodation.
Swimming and relaxing at Cambugahay Falls. In the afternoon, crowds thin out and you'll have the pools almost to yourself. Bring a picnic.

days_plan.itin_title: Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Visayas Circuit: Cebu, Bohol and Siquijor in 14 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days
Ultimate Philippines Grand Tour: Rice Terraces, Lagoons & Visayas in 21 Days

visits.text: Ferry from Cebu (Liloan port or Cebu City) to Tagbilaran, Bohol's capital. The crossing takes 2-4 hours depending on the ferry. Tagbilaran is a small provincial city with no major attractions, but it's the gateway to Bohol.

Baclayon Church, 6 km from Tagbilaran, is one of the Philippines' oldest - built of coral stone by Jesuits in 1596. The 2013 earthquake severely damaged it, but restoration is progressing. The adjacent museum displays religious artifacts from the colonial era.
The Loboc River winds through thick jungle in Bohol's interior. The banks are lined with coconut palms and stilt villages. It's one of the Philippines' greenest and most peaceful landscapes.

The Chocolate Hills are Bohol's natural landmark - 1,268 grass-covered conical hills that turn chocolate brown in the dry season (hence the name). From the Carmen viewpoint, the view is surreal: hundreds of identical domes stretch to the horizon like a giant model.

The Corella Tarsier Sanctuary is the only ethical place to observe these tiny primates (the world's smallest - they fit in your hand). Tarsiers are nocturnal and extremely sensitive to noise and light. The sanctuary enforces silence, prohibits flash photography, and limits visitor numbers. Respect the rules - stress can literally kill a tarsier.

The Man-Made Forest, a cathedral of mahogany trees planted in the 1950s, forms a green tunnel over 2 km of road between Loboc and Bilar. Light filters through the canopy - it's cool, quiet, and photogenic.

The bamboo hanging bridge at Sevilla spans the Loboc River 25 meters above the water. It sways when you walk across - fun or terrifying depending on your relationship with heights. The view of the river below is worth the vertigo.

Panglao, a small island connected to Bohol by two bridges, concentrates the beaches and resorts. Alona Beach, the most famous, is a curve of white sand bordered by restaurants and dive centers. It's Bohol's most developed beach - lively but not suffocating.

Hinagdanan Cave, in Panglao's interior, hides a freshwater underground lake beneath a stalactite ceiling. You descend by staircase into a cavity lit by natural light shafts. Swimming is possible - the water is cool and green.

Balicasag Island, 45 minutes by bangka from Panglao, is a marine sanctuary. The coral drop-off surrounding the island is one of the Visayas' most spectacular - the reef plunges vertically into inky blue 3 meters from shore.

activities.text: Lunch cruise on the Loboc River. A floating restaurant boat travels up the river for an hour, Filipino buffet on board, accompanied by local musicians. It's touristy but enjoyable - the river is beautiful and the food decent.
Alternative option: stand-up paddle or kayak on the Loboc River, more authentic than the cruise. You paddle beneath the canopy, encountering kingfishers and monitor lizards.

Scooter tour through Bohol's interior. The roads are decent, traffic almost non-existent outside towns. You pass rice paddies, village churches, coconut groves, and children shouting "Hey Joe!" (the nickname for foreigners since the war).
Dinner in Tagbilaran - try kalamay, a local confection made from glutinous rice and coconut sugar, sold in coconut shells. It's Bohol's edible souvenir.

Diving or snorkeling at Balicasag. Green turtles are everywhere, corals in excellent condition, and schools of surgeonfish form silver clouds at the drop-off edge. For certified divers, the descending wall is spectacular.
Dolphin watching off Panglao. Bangkas leave at dawn (5:30am) and regularly encounter groups of spinner dolphins. Not guaranteed, but frequent in dry season.
